RE: Mine Resistant Ambush Protected vehicle acquired through 1033 Program
To Whom It May Concern:
On April 29, 2014, the Benton County Sheriff’s Office obtained a Mine Resistant Ambush
Protected vehicle (MRAP) through Law Enforcement Support Office 1033 Program. As of
that date, our office has implemented a policy for the operation and training of the MRAP
vehicle. The purpose of obtaining this vehicle is for it to be utilized by the Benton County
Sheriffs Office Special Weapons and Tactics Team for the protection of officers and
citizens during high risk operations for the safety of persons from threats such as, but not
limited to potential gunfire.
The policy has guidelines for the operation and maintenance of the vehicle. A deputy that
has NOT received the appropriate training will not be allowed to operate the MRAP
vehicle, Furthermore, drivers will have (at a minimum) an Arkansas Class B Commercial
driver's license. The training will consist of the following:
v Overview of the vehicle to include its capabilities and limitations
v Characteristics of the vehicle so the operators will have knowledge of its size and
weight
Basic maintenance
Safe operating speeds
Movement and maneuver
Ground Guiding techniques for safe operation in small areas due to limited
visibility
Operation on slopes
Restricted terrain
Rough terrain driving
Vehicle tip and rollover
Steering and proper maneuvering
Mount and Dismount
Falls, smashed, and pinched extremities
Use of seatbelts
Risk of contact with low hanging objects such as power lines

PURPOSE:
The Benton County Sheriff's Office obtained a Mine Resistant Ambush Protected (MRAP)
Vehicle through the LESO 1033 Program. The purpose of this vehicle is that it will be utilized
by the Benton County Sheriff's Office for the protection of law enforcement officers and
citizens during high risk operations for the safety of persons from threats such as but not limited
to potential gunfire. Additional uses of this vehicle will be to assist citizens during natural
disaster events to navigate through extreme terrain or to drive through damaged areas to assist
citizens affected by such disasters.
1, Mine Resistant Ambush Protected Vehicle (MRAP)

operation while providing ballistic protection for the occupants.
li. | Assigned operators will keep an inventory of all equipment stored in the MRAP and
will notify the Team or Assistant Team Leader to replace items as needed due
to use.

outs and ensuring that it is maintained, well kept, and organized.

and alternate operators. Operators will also possess a valid Arkansas Class B
Commercial Driver’s License.
ii; Ground guide not required during tactical deployment; recommended under all other
conditions.

I.
The vehicle shall be secured from public access during storage. This can be
accomplished minimally by locking doors, turning off all power systems, and/or by

The vehicle will be kept maintained in a safe and sustainable operating condition and

unsafe for use shall require that the vehicle be taken out of service, tagged, and
notification made to the SWAT Commander and/or Operations Commander
immediately. The vehicle shall not be used until such time that it has been repaired
and/or rendered safe to operate.

Benton Police Department Policy:
Effective: 4/24/2015
Date of Origin: 4/24/2015
Armored Rescue Vehicle (ARV) Operations
I, Purpose
A. This policy provides clear guidelines for the safe and effective deployment of the Benton
Police Department’s Armored Rescue Vehicle (ARV).
[I]. Policy
A. It shall be the policy of the Benton Police Department to develop and implement a policy
that provides guidelines on the manner in which the ARV is deployed and maintained.
B. The policy will also contain guidelines that establish a mandated training program for all
personnel responsible for the ARV’s operational deployment. All department personnel
shall recognize and adhere to the guidelines set forth within this policy.
III. Procedures
A. ARV Deployment
l.
The Benton Police Department’s ARV is assigned as a tactical unit to the SWAT
team to be utilized in the mitigation of tactical situations requiring its use. More
specifically, the ARV is intended to be depioyed during tactical situations that require
additional protective measures for police officers, who have encountered or might
encounter gunfire or other hazardous situations during the performance of their
duties.
The Benton Police Department may also deploy the ARV to assist the citizens of our
Jurisdiction during natural disaster events that require the delivery of police services
or rescue measures, to include extreme terrain or high water events.
. In the event the ARV is deployed under the tenets of mutual aid, the Benton Police
Department will always provide personnel trained to operate the ARV. BNPD
personnel shall have the final decision regarding the safe and appropriate use of the
ARV. All requests for deployment of the ARV must be approved by the Chief of
Police or his designee.
B. ARV Operation
l.
The Benton Police Department’s ARV may only be operated by personnel within the
agency having been specifically trained to operate and maneuver the ARV in a safe
and effective manner. |

C. ARV Maintenance and Storage
1. The ARV will be maintained by personnel assigned to the SWAT Team. As such,
they shall ensure the ARV is serviced and maintained in accordance to the
manufacturer’s recommendations as required,
2. The ARV shall be maintained in an operationally ready status.

L Purpose: Mine Resistant Ambush Protected Vehicle (MRAP)
A vehicle designed to transport personnel and/or equipment to the scene of a tactical
operation while providing ballistic protection for the occupants.
Additional uses of this vehicle will be to assist the citizens of Cabot during natural
disaster events to navigate through extreme terrain or drive over damaged areas to
assist citizens affected by such disasters.
Il. Policy:
A. An inspection, to include running the motor while parked for one hour, will be
quarterly.
B. Asystem check and security check will be performed quarterly.
C. A pre-trip mspection prior to normal operation is required. This may be
disregarded under a tactical deployment.
D. A ground guide (Back-up spotter) is not required during tactical deployment but
recommended under all other conditions, including training.
EK. Code Three response to any deployment site is not authorized without the
approval of the Chief of Police or his designee.
F. An escort Police Unit will be utilized anytime the MRAP is in use while driving
any public roadway.
G. When not deployed or at a maintenance facility, the MRAP must be parked in an
area approved by the Chief of Police or his designee.
H. An eight (8) hour basic operator course will be required to operate the MRAP.
After the initial operator course is completed, all SORT Members/Operators will
be required an additional four (4) hours of annual training.

UI.
I. The Chief of Police or his designee will designate a SORT Member/Operator
responsible for driving the MRAP to cal]-outs, training and ensuring that it is
maintained, well-kept and organized.
J, SORT Team Leader and/or Assistant Team Leader will keep an inventory of all
equipment stored on or in the MRAP and will notify the SORT Team Commander
of items that need to be replaced as needed.
Training:
A. Basic pre-deployment safety and operation inspection.
B. Vehicle operation principles
Operating Speeds and Slope Restrictions:
|. MRAP vehicles have relatively quick acceleration and high top speeds for
vehicles of their weight and size. Most operations will not require use of all
the speed the MRAP vehicle can deliver. Its dash speed will permit rapid
movement between covered and concealed positions or when breaking
contact. Remember to avoid rapid steering movements, especially during
dashes,
2. Ultumately, the element leader will determine and set the rate of movement
based on thorough understanding of mission, enemy, terrain, weather and civil
considerations. Operating MRAP vehicles at high speed in a tactical
environment will get drivers into a dangerous situation faster than they can
counter or react to it.
3. Safety reminders while operating MRAP vehicles
a. Driving at high speed does not increase safety or security during tactical
operations. Higher speeds reduce driver reaction time, extend stopping
distances and increase roll rates that can lead to tip over/rollover.
b. Traveling at high speeds (anything preater than 25 mph) significantly
decrease the ability to accurately visually scan the road surface and
shoulders for hazards.
c, Slow speeds and careful observations greatly increase the chance of
spotting hazards,
d. MRAP vehicles are generally very tall with a high center of gravity, which
greatly increases the chance of a rollover/tip over. Slow speeds will
reduce the possibility of an accident.
C. Offroad operations
D. Vehicle safety features
EK. Emergency vehicle shutdown procedures
F. Annual monitored vehicle driving examination, to include vehicle start-up,
forward motion, reverse motion, turns, proper vehicle shutdown and verbal
review of off-road operations and emergency procedures.

G. Fire Drill
1, Crew announces “FIRE!” and gives the location;
a. Crew Compartment
b. Engine Compartment
2. Crew waits for vehicle to stop moving.
3. Crew checks for casualties and uses fire blanket if equipped and/or required
4. Crew secures weapons and gear (if able).
5. Driver turns vehicle off.
6. Vehicle commander notifies Chain of Command of the incident.
7. Crew uses available fire extinguishers to suppress fire.
8. Vehicle commander orders “Bail out!” and gives direction to the rally point.
9. All personnel exit the vehicle, a head count is completed by the vehicle
commander upon exit
10. Crew and personnel meet at designated rally point.
IV. Driving Course: The listed driving course is a familiarization with the MRAP and
shall be practiced and documented by each operator annually. All documented
training on the MRAP will be forwarded to the Team Commander and the Cabot
Police Department Training Officer.

To Whom it May Concern
As of the 29" day of May 2015, the Craighead County Sheriff's Office has implemented a policy
of the operation and training of the MRAP vehicle obtained through the LESO/1033 program. The
purpose of this vehicle is to be utilized by the Craighead County Special Weapons and Tactics team for
the protection of officers during high risk operations for the safety of such officers frorn potential
gunfire while in the performance of their duties, Additional uses of this vehicle will be to assist the
citizens of Craighead County during natural disasters events to navigate through extreme terrain or drive
over damaged areas to assist citizens affected by such disasters,
The policy also has guidelines for the operation and maintenance of the vehicle. It states that no officer
will be allowed to operate the vehicle without such training. The training at this time is conducted by a
trained deputy with this department who has received training in the use and Operation by Smith
Integrated Technology M.R.A.P. Logistical Sustainment Group. All training curriculum used in training Is
derived from the Center for Army Lessons Learned published by Maneuver Center of Excellence at Fort
Benning, titled MRAP Observations, Insight, and Lessons and BAE Systems MRAP Instructor Guide. This
training will consist of the following:
* Overview of the vehicle to include its capabilities and limitations
e Characteristics of the vehicle so the operators will have knowledge of its size and weight.
e Basic maintenance
* Safe operating speed
* Movement and maneuvers
* Ground guiding techniques for safe operation in small areas due to limited visibility
® Operations on slopes
* Restricted terrain
e® Rough terrain driving
® Vehicle tip and rollover
* Steering and proper maneuvering

* Falls, smashed and pinched extremities
« Rollover/egress procedures
* Risks and contact with high voltage power lines
These items will include classroom portions and a hands on / driver training portion.
Capt. Justin Rolland, Asst. Chief Deputy

Mine Resistant Ambush Protected (MRAP) Vehicle 
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) 
 
Effective Date:  January 13, 2015 
Revisions:  None 
 
PURPOSE: 
 
The Jefferson County Sheriff’s Office obtained a Mine Resistant Ambush Protected (MRAP) 
Vehicle through the LESO 1033 Program.  The Jefferson County Sheriff recognizes and 
appreciates the uniqueness of this vehicle.  The purpose of this vehicle is that it will be utilized 
by the Jefferson County Sheriff’s Office for the protection of law enforcement officers and 
citizens during high risk operations for the safety of persons from threats such as but not 
limited to potential gunfire.  Additional uses of this vehicle will be to assist citizens during 
natural disaster events to navigate through extreme terrain or to drive through damaged areas 
to assist citizens affected by such disasters. 
 
MAINTENANCE AND STORAGE: 
 
1. The vehicle shall be secured from public access during storage.  This can be 
accomplished minimally by locking doors, turning off all power systems, and/or by 
storage in a secure building or structure from public. 
2. The vehicle will be kept maintained in a safe and sustainable operating condition and 
will be in a response capable condition at all times. 
3. Any conditions identified during inspection and maintenance that may render the 
vehicle unsafe for use shall require that the vehicle be taken out of service, tagged, and 
notification made to the SRT Commander and/or Operations Commander immediately.  
The vehicle shall not be used until such time that it has been repaired and/or rendered 
safe to operate.  
 
TRAINING AND OPERATION: 
 
1. No officer will be allowed to operate the vehicle without training.

2. The training may be completed by in-service staff instructors, who have completed a 
train-the-trainer course, or by a contracted company or trained military member who 
has extensive experience or qualifications with this type of vehicle. 
3. Training shall include classroom instruction and a hands on / drivers training 
components. 
4. The training shall at a minimum consist of the following: 
• Overview of the vehicle to include its capabilities and limitations. 
• Characteristics of the vehicle so the operators will have a knowledge of its size and 
weight. 
• Basic Preventative Maintenance Checks & Services (PMCS). 
• Safe operating speeds. 
• Steering, movement and maneuvering. 
• Visibility concerns. 
• Operation of controls and indicators. 
• Ground guide techniques for safe operation. 
• Operation on slopes. 
• Restricted terrain. 
• Rollover hazards and egress procedures. 
• Mount and dismount. 
• Falls, smashed, and pinched extremities. 
• Use of seatbelts. 
• Risks of contact with low hanging objects such as power lines.

I. Purpose
A. The magnitude of demands for police service increases as today’s society becomes
more mobile and affluent. It becomes more evident that rapid mobility, providing
swiftness of response, in all areas of police service is essential. The growing
complexity of police work has resulted in an increase in specialization and some
modification in the previously accepted concept of patrol duties. The successful
implementation of the preventative patrol concept requires adaptation of new
techniques to our patrol efforts.
B. The objectives of the program are directly related to the insertion of helicopters
into the patrol function. The following examples serve to emphasize the desired
objective:
1. Supporting and coordinating patrols and the protection of life and
property;
2. Traffic control and surveillance;
3. Crime prevention; |
4, Criminal surveillance;
5. Search and rescue;
6. Special surveillance details;
7. Apprehension of offenders; and,
8. Cover for ground elements.
C. The above examples of objectives to be accomplished have one factor in common.
Cooperation between ground units and aircraft is essential. The capabilities of the
patrol car and aircraft can do much to achieve these goals only when they are used
in a coordinated effort to support each other. It is evident, therefore, that the
success of this program requires the active support of every member of our
Department.
D. Accountability - The Aviation Unit is supervised by the Aviation Unit Sergeant.
The Aviation Unit Sergeant is also the Special Events Coordinator who is directly
responsible to the Aviation Unit Lieutenant, who is the Special Units Commander.
The Aviation Unit Lieutenant is directly responsible to the Headquarters Division
Commander.

C) The capability of helicopters to operate from unimproved areas
is recognized and as a general policy, helicopters may be
operated anywhere within the city limits, however, the pilot has
the responsibility for adherence to appropriate regulations and
use of caution and sound judgment while operating over the
City.
3. Landing and Take-Off Areas
a) Aircraft may use any designated area for operating at airports
when the control towers are in operation. When towers are not in
operation, helicopters will utilize only the designated landing
areas. Wind direction will dictate which pad to land on.
b) An emergency involving safety of personnel or equipment may
require immediate actions and decisions that may deviate from
appropriate regulations.
C. Weather
l. Weather Minimums
a) Visual Flight Rules (VFR). Flights may be conducted under
VFR only when the ceiling is 800 feet or more and the visibility
is two miles or more within the control zone and both are
forecast to remain for the duration of flights.
b) Special VFR Flight. The purpose of a special VFR clearance is
to permit entry into, or departure from an area below VFR
minimum. A special VFR clearance must be requested from the
tower before entering or departing the control zone. (Exception:
Helicopter visibility is 4% statute mile for daylight operation and
one (1) statute mile for night time operation.)
Headquarters Division 5600-12-12 Divisional Operating Procedure

c) Visual Flight Rule Minimum
(1)Remain clear of clouds and maintain visual contact with the
ground.
(2) Helicopter flights are restricted from VFR flights at night, unless
a visible horizon or suitable reference is apparent outside
the cockpit.
2. Maximum Wind Conditions
a) Maximum wind velocities for aircraft operations are established
below for aircraft assigned to the Aviation Unit. The aircraft
will not be required to be flown on regular patrol when winds
equal or exceed the winds specified in the aircraft
manufacturer’s handbook. Critical crosswind components will
be determined from the pilot’s handbook.
b) Although “Down For Wind” conditions do not necessarily
prohibit the answering of radio calls, whether or not to respond
will be at the discretion of the pilot.
D. Hangaring Aircraft
1. All helicopters will be placed in the hangar when:
a) Any type weather conditions prohibit flight. This will be done
immediately by on-duty flight personnel.
b) Outside temperature falls to 20 degrees or lower, unless the
aircraft is to be used within one hour.
Cc) It will not be used on the next shift. This will be the
responsibility of the off going crew.
2. Ground Handling
a) A minimum ground crew of two will be used to hangar the
helicopter for safety reasons.
b) Helicopters may be towed in or out of the hangar with the
tractor, provided proper caution and a ground guide is used. All
aircraft will be backed, tail first, into the hangar.
C) Caution should be used when placing the tow dolly under the
helicopter. Ascertain that it is properly aligned to prevent cross
tube and skid damage.
E. Aircraft Radio Procedures
l. All aircraft, when operating within the boundaries of Adams Field, will
monitor the appropriate radio frequency at all times.
Headquarters Division 5600-12-13 Divisional Operating Procedure

2. In the event of loss of communications during SVFR, flights shall be
expected to proceed directly to the nearest suitable landing site, and then
advise Adams Tower by telephone.
F, Refueling and Service Procedures
1, Refueling Procedures
a) Aircraft to be fueled will be located a safe minimum distance
from buildings, structures, and any other probable sources of
ignition. The aircraft will be grounded prior to refueling.
b) Aircraft will only be refueled with the engine running in
emergency situations (“Hot” Refueling). The pilot retains the
authority to prohibit “Hot” refueling under any situation,
including emergencies.
) No person will remain in any aircraft while it is being refueled,
(except emergency “Hot” refueling).
d) The observer should assist in refueling the aircraft.
G. Duties and Responsibilities of the Maintenance Coordinator/Mechanic
l. The Maintenance Coordinator/Mechanic shall be under the direct
command of the Aviation Unit Commander. The Maintenance
Coordinator/Mechanic is responsible, but not limited to the following:
a) The Maintenance Coordinator/Mechanic shall be a licensed
Airframe and Power plant mechanic. He shall attend the
recommended factory maintenance schools for any aircraft that
he is required to maintain for the City that he does not have
adequate maintenance experience with; The purpose of the
Maintenance Coordinator/t Mechanic is to perform maintenance
on aircraft airframe and power plant assemblies and to certify
the airworthiness of the aircraft after maintenance has been
performed.
b) He/She shall perform, or advise the Unit Commander to have
performed, all necessary scheduled or unscheduled maintenance
and/or inspections as required; All maintenance will be
performed by an FAA certified A & P mechanic.
C) Verifies that all required inspections and maintenance has been
performed .
d) He shall insure the aircraft engine and airframe logbooks are
maintained and the proper entries are made in a timely manner.
e) Services and repairs aircraft using acceptable maintenance
practices and procedures that ensure the aircraft remain in
airworthiness condition.
Headquarters Division 5600-12-14 Divisional Operating Procedure

f) Performs aircraft inspections as required and necessary to ensure
that aircraft remain in airworthiness condition and communicates
with the pilots and observers to determine if they have
discovered any maintenance problems with the aircraft;
g) The Maintenance Coordinator/Mechanic has the authority to
Ground Run the aircraft to complete any maintenance checks
that might be required as the result of any maintenance he has
performed.
h) Assists the Aviation Unit Sergeant and Observers in maintaining
a written parts inventory.
i) Performs other duties as required.
2. Nothing herein is intended to suggest the above described responsibilities
are solely the responsibility of the Maintenance Coordinator/Mechanic.
Pilots and observers assigned to the Unit will assist him with any or all of
his duties as may be required to fulfill the duties of his assignment,
without violating any Federal Aviation Regulation.
H. Safety
l. Smoking and Wearing of the Shoulder Safety Harness
a) Smoking is prohibited within fifty (50) feet of fueling operations
and helicopters.
b) Smoking is prohibited in the hangar area in accordance with
current Department Directives.
c) Smoking is prohibited in the aircraft.
d) If the helicopter is equipped with a shoulder safety harness, it
will be used at all times.
2. Uniforms
a) The cleanliness and correct fit of uniforms is necessary to the
proper appearance of all Police Aviation Unit personnel. The
Unit supervisors will ensure that each member of the Aviation
Unit is properly fitted with neat and correct uniforms.
b) The flight suits are the duty uniforms for Aviation Unit
personnel.
Cc) Flight suits may not be worn to funerals and may not be worn to
court.
d) Helmets or headsets WILL BE worn during all helicopter flights.
[. Administration
1. Medical Examinations
Headquarters Division 5600-12-15 Divisional Operating Procedure

K. Miscellaneous
1. No officer will fire any weapon from the aircraft.
2. Doors may be removed from the aircraft only with an Aviation Unit’s
supervisor’s approval. If removal for an emergency reason should
become necessary, the Quick Release Pin should remain with the door.
Proper care should be taken in removing and placing the doors in a
secured area to prevent any damage. Appropriate airspeed limitations
will be complied with.
V. Procedures for Radio Service Calls
A. Aviation Unit personnel shall respond to all requests for service made by members
of the Police Department.
B. When on regular patrol, Aviation Unit personnel will respond to any call where the
aircraft can be of use to the officers on the ground or assist in the arrest of a
suspect.
C. Effective communications is essential with the officers on the ground.
VI. Reports
A. Shift Summary Reports are due daily as needed upon mission activity or an
emergency call-out basis detailing the flight(s), specific activity conducted, etc...
VII. Annual Review
A. The Sergeant of the Aviation Unit will do an annual review of the Unit.
B. The Annual Report is due in December with the other end of the year reports for
the Headquarters Division.
C. An assessment will be made to determine the effectiveness of the program and the
need for additional Aviation Unit personnel.
Headquarters Division 5600-12-17 Divisional Operating Procedure

The Mississippi County Sheriff's Department has received a Mine Resistant Ambush Protected
Vehicle (MRAP} via the LESO 1033 program. The MRAP will be utilized by the Special Response team of
the Mississippi County Sheriff's Department for the protection of its officers during high risk operations
where such officers may be subjected to gunfire while in the performance of their duties. The vehicle
will also be utilized for other purposes such as significant weather events and natural disasters where
the off road capabilities of the vehicle can be utilized to assist the citizens of Mississippi County during
such disasters. The vehicle may also be used in public relations events to allow the public to observe the
capabilities of the vehicle.
The Mississippi County Sheriff's Department has also implemented guidelines and training for
the operation and maintenance of the vehicle. No officer will be allowed be allowed to operate the
vehicle until he or she has completed a training course derived from military technical manuals and
training materials and manufacturers manuals and training materials on the operation and maintenance
of the vehicte.
Training topics are included but not limited to the following:
* An overview of the vehicle’s capabilities and limitations.
® Characteristics of the vehicle so that operators have knowledge of its size and weight.
¢ Basic Maintenance
e Safe operation speeds and steering maneuvers
¢ Use of Ground guides due to limited visibility
e Operation on slopes

RE: MRAP
James:
As of 6/1/15, the Pulaski County Sheriff's Office (PCSO) has implemented a policy for the operation and
training of the Mine-Resistant Ambush Protected (MRAP) vehicle obtained from the Law Enforcement Support
Office (LESO). The purpose of the vehicle is for use by the PCSO Special Weapons and Tactics (SWAT) Team in
the protection of personnel during high risk operations by providing safe cover from potential gunfire while in the
performance of their duties. Additional uses of this vehicle are to assist the citizens of Pulaski County during
natural disaster events to navigate through extreme terrain or traverse over er damaged areas when aiding citizens
affected d by such disasters.
The policy contains guidelines for the operation and maintenance of the vehicle, based upon curriculum
used in training established by the Center for Army Lessons Learned and published by the Combined Arms Center
at Fort Leavenworth, Kansas, titled MRAP Vehicle Tactics, Techniques and Procedures. The training will consist of
the following:
1. Overview of the vehicle to include capabilities and limitations.
2. Vehicle characteristics.
3. Maintenance.
4. Safe operating speeds.
5. Vehicle steering, movement and proper maneuvering.
6. Ground guiding techniques due to limited visibility.
7. Operations of slopes.
8. Restricted terrain.
9. Rough terrain driving.
10. Vehicle tip over and roll-over, to include egress procedures.
This training will include both classroom and practical hands-on exercises.
